lines.line: # Introduction and Consent

> The interviewer, Dr. Fiona Vernal, introduces herself and the purpose of the oral history interview. The interviewee, Larissa Canada, provides her full name and gives explicit permission for the interview to be conducted. The conversation begins with a brief emotional reflection as the speaker prepares to share her memories.

metadata.title: Introduction and Consent
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer, Dr. Fiona Vernal, introduces herself and the purpose of the oral history interview. The interviewee, Larissa Canada, provides her full name and gives explicit permission for the interview to be conducted. The conversation begins with a brief emotional reflection as the speaker prepares to share her memories.

lines.line: # Moving from Bellevue Square to Oakland Terrace and Family Reflections

> The speaker recalls the emotional experience of moving from Bellevue Square to Oakland Terrace in 1967. She describes her large family of 15 siblings and the happiness they found in their close-knit environment, despite being labeled as 'poor' in an article. The speaker emphasizes the richness of their family bonds and the adaptability required to thrive in small living quarters.

metadata.title: Moving from Bellevue Square to Oakland Terrace and Family Reflections
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recalls the emotional experience of moving from Bellevue Square to Oakland Terrace in 1967. She describes her large family of 15 siblings and the happiness they found in their close-knit environment, despite being labeled as 'poor' in an article. The speaker emphasizes the richness of their family bonds and the adaptability required to thrive in small living quarters.

lines.line: # The Newspaper Article and Leaving Bellevue Square

> The conversation centers on a newspaper article from December 18, 1967, which chronicled the family's move from Bellevue Square to Oakland Terrace. The speaker discusses the involvement of the Hartford Housing Authority and their church, Saint Michael's Roman Catholic Church, in facilitating the move. She reflects on the excitement of moving to a larger home, the bittersweet feeling of leaving friends and a unified community, and the significance of the transition for her family.

metadata.title: The Newspaper Article and Leaving Bellevue Square
metadata.synopsis: The conversation centers on a newspaper article from December 18, 1967, which chronicled the family's move from Bellevue Square to Oakland Terrace. The speaker discusses the involvement of the Hartford Housing Authority and their church, Saint Michael's Roman Catholic Church, in facilitating the move. She reflects on the excitement of moving to a larger home, the bittersweet feeling of leaving friends and a unified community, and the significance of the transition for her family.

lines.line: # Family Background and Heritage

> The speaker shares that she was 14 years old at the time of the move and provides background on her parents. Her mother came to Hartford as a child from Georgia, experiencing the flood of 1938, while her father was from Middletown. The family, all born in Bellevue Square, was raised with strong values and a sense of unity, with the mother playing a central role in raising the children.

metadata.title: Family Background and Heritage
metadata.synopsis: The speaker shares that she was 14 years old at the time of the move and provides background on her parents. Her mother came to Hartford as a child from Georgia, experiencing the flood of 1938, while her father was from Middletown. The family, all born in Bellevue Square, was raised with strong values and a sense of unity, with the mother playing a central role in raising the children.

lines.line: # Parental Occupations and Family Dynamics

> The speaker describes her father's work as a bricklayer and the excitement the children felt visiting his job sites. She explains the family dynamic, noting that all siblings were single births and that her mother, deeply religious and involved in the Catholic Church, viewed each child as a gift from God. The family adapted to their circumstances and found joy in their large household.

metadata.title: Parental Occupations and Family Dynamics
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes her father's work as a bricklayer and the excitement the children felt visiting his job sites. She explains the family dynamic, noting that all siblings were single births and that her mother, deeply religious and involved in the Catholic Church, viewed each child as a gift from God. The family adapted to their circumstances and found joy in their large household.

lines.line: # Grandparents and Family History

> The discussion turns to the speaker's grandparents, including her limited memories of her maternal grandmother and more direct experiences with her paternal grandmother, who helped care for the children during births. The speaker learns from her older siblings that her maternal grandfather, Henry Brown Sr., may have helped build Bellevue Square, though documentation is lacking. She also provides names and details about other ancestors, highlighting the importance of family history.

metadata.title: Grandparents and Family History
metadata.synopsis: The discussion turns to the speaker's grandparents, including her limited memories of her maternal grandmother and more direct experiences with her paternal grandmother, who helped care for the children during births. The speaker learns from her older siblings that her maternal grandfather, Henry Brown Sr., may have helped build Bellevue Square, though documentation is lacking. She also provides names and details about other ancestors, highlighting the importance of family history.

lines.line: # Family Reunions and the Importance of Family History

> The speaker reflects on the decline of large family reunions since 1997, attributing it to family members moving away and the passing of older generations. She expresses a sense of responsibility to keep the family history alive for younger generations and discusses the importance of reunions in maintaining connections. The conversation touches on the impact of her mother's organizational skills and the need to preserve family traditions and stories.

metadata.title: Family Reunions and the Importance of Family History
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the decline of large family reunions since 1997, attributing it to family members moving away and the passing of older generations. She expresses a sense of responsibility to keep the family history alive for younger generations and discusses the importance of reunions in maintaining connections. The conversation touches on the impact of her mother's organizational skills and the need to preserve family traditions and stories.

lines.line: # Childhood in Bellevue Square: Community and Daily Life

> The speaker describes her early years in Bellevue Square, including the apartments her family lived in and the close-knit nature of the community. She recalls attending Arsenal School, the sense of unity among large families, and the abundance of amenities within the housing project. The environment fostered strong friendships and a sense of belonging, with everything the family needed located nearby.

metadata.title: Childhood in Bellevue Square: Community and Daily Life
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes her early years in Bellevue Square, including the apartments her family lived in and the close-knit nature of the community. She recalls attending Arsenal School, the sense of unity among large families, and the abundance of amenities within the housing project. The environment fostered strong friendships and a sense of belonging, with everything the family needed located nearby.

lines.line: # Miss Gwendolyn Reed and Community Influences

> The speaker highlights the profound influence of Miss Gwendolyn Reed, a community activist, actress, and organizer who played a pivotal role in the lives of Bellevue Square residents. Miss Reed opened her home to children, inspiring the speaker's interest in puppetry and community service. Her involvement in the Negro Theater, Head Start, and local events left a lasting impression, encouraging the speaker to value books, education, and community engagement.

metadata.title: Miss Gwendolyn Reed and Community Influences
metadata.synopsis: The speaker highlights the profound influence of Miss Gwendolyn Reed, a community activist, actress, and organizer who played a pivotal role in the lives of Bellevue Square residents. Miss Reed opened her home to children, inspiring the speaker's interest in puppetry and community service. Her involvement in the Negro Theater, Head Start, and local events left a lasting impression, encouraging the speaker to value books, education, and community engagement.

lines.line: # Community Layout, Amenities, and Local Businesses

> The conversation shifts to the physical layout of Bellevue Square, which featured multi-level apartment buildings, a community center, playgrounds, and local businesses. The speaker recalls the convenience of having a drugstore (Jake's) and a candy store (Nellie's), both run by Black proprietors, as well as informal food vendors. The self-contained nature of the community meant residents rarely needed to leave for daily necessities.

metadata.title: Community Layout, Amenities, and Local Businesses
metadata.synopsis: The conversation shifts to the physical layout of Bellevue Square, which featured multi-level apartment buildings, a community center, playgrounds, and local businesses. The speaker recalls the convenience of having a drugstore (Jake's) and a candy store (Nellie's), both run by Black proprietors, as well as informal food vendors. The self-contained nature of the community meant residents rarely needed to leave for daily necessities.

lines.line: # Family Connections and Local Families

> The interviewer asks about the Barlow family, to which the speaker confirms a familial connection. She explains how the Barlows are related through her mother's side and shares memories of specific family members. The discussion underscores the interconnectedness of families within Bellevue Square and the generational ties that shaped the community.

metadata.title: Family Connections and Local Families
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer asks about the Barlow family, to which the speaker confirms a familial connection. She explains how the Barlows are related through her mother's side and shares memories of specific family members. The discussion underscores the interconnectedness of families within Bellevue Square and the generational ties that shaped the community.

lines.line: # School Experiences and Teachers

> The speaker reminisces about her love of reading and time spent at the Main Street library. She fondly remembers her teachers at Arsenal School, particularly Miss Mildred Smith and Miss West, who were among the first African American teachers she encountered. Their encouragement and high expectations left a lasting impact, fostering a lifelong passion for learning and education.

metadata.title: School Experiences and Teachers
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reminisces about her love of reading and time spent at the Main Street library. She fondly remembers her teachers at Arsenal School, particularly Miss Mildred Smith and Miss West, who were among the first African American teachers she encountered. Their encouragement and high expectations left a lasting impact, fostering a lifelong passion for learning and education.

lines.line: # Community Elders and Church Life

> The speaker recalls other influential community members, such as Mr. Smith from Parks and Recreation, who ensured children had access to programs and equipment. She describes the family's regular church attendance at Saint Michael's and Saint Benedict, the importance of religious education, and the role of her mother in maintaining spiritual and moral guidance. The sense of community extended to shared discipline and mutual support among families.

metadata.title: Community Elders and Church Life
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recalls other influential community members, such as Mr. Smith from Parks and Recreation, who ensured children had access to programs and equipment. She describes the family's regular church attendance at Saint Michael's and Saint Benedict, the importance of religious education, and the role of her mother in maintaining spiritual and moral guidance. The sense of community extended to shared discipline and mutual support among families.

lines.line: # Transition to Northwest Catholic and Navigating Difference

> The speaker discusses her transition from Bellevue Square to attending Northwest Catholic, a predominantly white school in West Hartford. She describes the challenges of feeling different due to race and socioeconomic status, the impact of the civil rights era, and her initial reluctance to leave her familiar environment. Despite her fears, she recognizes the opportunity for personal growth and advancement.

metadata.title: Transition to Northwest Catholic and Navigating Difference
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses her transition from Bellevue Square to attending Northwest Catholic, a predominantly white school in West Hartford. She describes the challenges of feeling different due to race and socioeconomic status, the impact of the civil rights era, and her initial reluctance to leave her familiar environment. Despite her fears, she recognizes the opportunity for personal growth and advancement.

lines.line: # Experiences at Northwest Catholic: Challenges and Friendships

> The speaker elaborates on her experiences at Northwest Catholic, detailing the difficulties of fitting in and the support she received from a Black Spanish teacher who recognized her potential. Over time, she formed meaningful friendships with both white and Black students, some of whom visited her home in Hartford. These relationships helped her navigate the complexities of being between two worlds and prepared her for future academic and social challenges.

metadata.title: Experiences at Northwest Catholic: Challenges and Friendships
metadata.synopsis: The speaker elaborates on her experiences at Northwest Catholic, detailing the difficulties of fitting in and the support she received from a Black Spanish teacher who recognized her potential. Over time, she formed meaningful friendships with both white and Black students, some of whom visited her home in Hartford. These relationships helped her navigate the complexities of being between two worlds and prepared her for future academic and social challenges.

lines.line: # Life Beyond Bellevue Square: Venturing Out and Family Routines

> The conversation turns to the family's routines outside Bellevue Square, such as grocery shopping trips in the family station wagon and outings to Keney Park for picnics. The speaker describes the embarrassment and humor associated with being part of a large family and the importance of these shared experiences. She notes that the family rarely needed to leave Bellevue Square due to the abundance of local amenities.

metadata.title: Life Beyond Bellevue Square: Venturing Out and Family Routines
metadata.synopsis: The conversation turns to the family's routines outside Bellevue Square, such as grocery shopping trips in the family station wagon and outings to Keney Park for picnics. The speaker describes the embarrassment and humor associated with being part of a large family and the importance of these shared experiences. She notes that the family rarely needed to leave Bellevue Square due to the abundance of local amenities.

lines.line: # College at Eastern and Personal Growth

> The speaker recounts her college experience at Eastern, where she participated in a summer program for African American students. She describes the personal growth that came from living away from her family, overcoming challenges such as a roommate who refused to live with her due to race, and excelling academically. Her time at Eastern solidified her desire to become a teacher and inspired her to give back to her community through education and mentorship.

metadata.title: College at Eastern and Personal Growth
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recounts her college experience at Eastern, where she participated in a summer program for African American students. She describes the personal growth that came from living away from her family, overcoming challenges such as a roommate who refused to live with her due to race, and excelling academically. Her time at Eastern solidified her desire to become a teacher and inspired her to give back to her community through education and mentorship.

lines.line: # Reflections on Project Life and Lasting Lessons

> The speaker reflects on the valuable life lessons learned from growing up in a housing project like Bellevue Square. She emphasizes the richness of experience, the importance of cooperation, and the pride she feels in her background. Her experiences shaped her commitment to lifelong learning, community service, and the belief that coming from humble beginnings can be a source of strength and hope.

metadata.title: Reflections on Project Life and Lasting Lessons

She emphasizes the richness of experience, the importance of cooperation, and the pride she feels in her background. Her experiences shaped her commitment to lifelong learning, community service, and the belief that coming from humble beginnings can be a source of strength and hope.

lines.line: # Wrapping Up and Planning for Part Two

> The interview concludes with the interviewer assigning homework to the speaker, suggesting a follow-up session to continue documenting her story. The speaker is encouraged to gather additional family information and involve other relatives in future interviews. Both express the importance of preserving these oral histories for future generations and maintaining the legacy of Bellevue Square and its community.

lines.line: # Moving from Bellevue Square to Oakland Terrace and Family Reflections

> The speaker reflects emotionally on her family's move from Bellevue Square to Oakland Terrace in 1967. She describes the happiness and unity of her large family of 15, emphasizing that despite being labeled as poor, she felt rich due to the love and support within the family. The memories are triggered by old photographs, contrasting joyful and sad occasions, such as the passing of her mother, and highlighting the blessings and changes over the years.

She recalls the excitement and challenges of leaving behind friends and a close-knit community, as well as the anticipation of having more space and a new home.

lines.line: # Family Life in Bellevue Square

> The speaker describes life in Bellevue Square, emphasizing the strong sense of community and unity among residents. She recounts how large families, including her own, bonded and supported each other, making the small living quarters feel rich with love and togetherness. The speaker shares anecdotes about adapting to limited space, eating in shifts, and the joy of finally having a large table after moving, likening their family to the Waltons.

The speaker highlights her mother's religious devotion and the significance of having a large family, with all siblings born in Bellevue Square.

lines.line: # Grandparents and Family History

> The speaker discusses her grandparents on both sides, sharing memories and family stories. She recalls her paternal grandmother's role during her mother's hospital stays and mentions not knowing her maternal grandmother personally. The conversation reveals that her maternal grandfather, Henry Brown Sr., helped build Bellevue Square, a fact passed down by her oldest sister. The speaker also lists family names and reflects on inheriting the role of family historian.

metadata.title: Transition to Northwest Catholic and Navigating Difference
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes her transition from Bellevue Square to attending Northwest Catholic, a predominantly white and affluent school. She discusses the challenges of feeling different due to race and socioeconomic status, the initial difficulties in adjusting, and the support she received from teachers. The experience broadened her perspective, strengthened her resolve to become a teacher, and highlighted the importance of seizing educational opportunities.

lines.line: # Making Friends and Navigating Race at Northwest Catholic

> The speaker shares her experiences making friends at Northwest Catholic, including both white and Black students. She describes how some white classmates became genuine friends, visiting each other's homes despite societal norms. The speaker also discusses the racial dynamics of the time, the support from a Black Spanish teacher who encouraged her, and the impact of these relationships on her confidence and academic journey.

metadata.title: Making Friends and Navigating Race at Northwest Catholic

She describes how some white classmates became genuine friends, visiting each other's homes despite societal norms. The speaker also discusses the racial dynamics of the time, the support from a Black Spanish teacher who encouraged her, and the impact of these relationships on her confidence and academic journey.

lines.line: # Life Beyond Bellevue Square: Shopping, Transportation, and Family Outings

> The conversation shifts to life outside Bellevue Square, with the speaker describing limited excursions beyond the neighborhood due to the self-sufficiency of the community. She recalls family shopping trips in their station wagon, feelings of embarrassment as a child, and family outings to parks during hot summers. These experiences highlight the family's resourcefulness and the importance of togetherness in their daily lives.

metadata.title: Life Beyond Bellevue Square: Shopping, Transportation, and Family Outings

These experiences highlight the family's resourcefulness and the importance of togetherness in their daily lives.

lines.line: # College at Eastern and Personal Growth

> The speaker discusses her transition to college at Eastern, where she participated in a summer program for students with potential. She describes the personal growth she experienced, the challenges of being away from her close-knit family, and early encounters with racism, such as a white roommate refusing to share a room. These experiences helped her develop resilience, a sense of purpose, and a commitment to education and community service.

metadata.title: College at Eastern and Personal Growth

lines.line: # Giving Back and Reflections on Lessons from Bellevue Square

> The speaker reflects on her ongoing commitment to giving back to the community, inspired by her upbringing in Bellevue Square. She emphasizes the valuable life lessons learned from growing up in a large family with limited resources, such as cooperation, adaptability, and hope. The speaker expresses pride in her background and the positive impact it has had on her life and career as an educator and community volunteer.

lines.line: # Wrapping Up and Planning for Future Interviews

> The interview concludes with Dr. Vernal assigning homework and planning for a follow-up session. The speaker is encouraged to gather more family information and involve siblings in future interviews. Both express enthusiasm for documenting and preserving the history of Bellevue Square and the speaker's family, recognizing the importance of oral history in capturing community experiences.

children.text: # Introduction and Consent

> The interview begins with Dr. Fiona Vernal introducing herself and the purpose of the oral history project. The speaker, Larissa Canada, provides her full name and grants permission for the interview. The initial moments set a respectful and formal tone for the conversation, establishing the context and consent necessary for the oral history.
# Moving from Bellevue Square to Oakland Terrace and Family Reflections

> The speaker reflects emotionally on her family's move from Bellevue Square to Oakland Terrace in 1967. She describes the happiness and unity of her large family of 15, emphasizing that despite being labeled as poor, she felt rich due to the love and support within the family. The memories are triggered by old photographs, contrasting joyful and sad occasions, such as the passing of her mother, and highlighting the blessings and changes over the years.
# The Newspaper Article and the Move

> The conversation centers on a newspaper article from December 18, 1967, which documented the family's move from Bellevue Square to Oakland Terrace. The speaker discusses the role of the Hartford Housing Authority and Saint Michael's Roman Catholic Church in facilitating the move. She recalls the excitement and challenges of leaving behind friends and a close-knit community, as well as the anticipation of having more space and a new home.
# Family Life in Bellevue Square

> The speaker describes life in Bellevue Square, emphasizing the strong sense of community and unity among residents. She recounts how large families, including her own, bonded and supported each other, making the small living quarters feel rich with love and togetherness. The speaker shares anecdotes about adapting to limited space, eating in shifts, and the joy of finally having a large table after moving, likening their family to the Waltons.
# Parents' Background and Family Heritage

> The discussion shifts to the speaker's age during the move and delves into her parents' backgrounds. Her mother came to Hartford from Georgia as a child and experienced the flood of 1938, while her father was from Middletown and worked as a bricklayer. The speaker highlights her mother's religious devotion and the significance of having a large family, with all siblings born in Bellevue Square.
# Grandparents and Family History

> The speaker discusses her grandparents on both sides, sharing memories and family stories. She recalls her paternal grandmother's role during her mother's hospital stays and mentions not knowing her maternal grandmother personally. The conversation reveals that her maternal grandfather, Henry Brown Sr., helped build Bellevue Square, a fact passed down by her oldest sister. The speaker also lists family names and reflects on inheriting the role of family historian.
# Family Reunions and the Importance of Family History

> The topic turns to family reunions, with the speaker explaining why large gatherings have become less frequent due to siblings moving away and the passing of family members. She expresses a sense of responsibility to keep the family connected and preserve their history for future generations. The speaker emphasizes the importance of reunions in maintaining family bonds and passing down stories, especially as the number of living siblings decreases.
# Childhood in Bellevue Square: School, Community, and Influential Figures

> The speaker recounts her childhood in Bellevue Square, describing the apartments, school experiences, and the vibrant community life. She highlights the influence of community leader Miss Gwendolyn Reed, who was an actress and organizer, and whose home served as a source of inspiration. The speaker also discusses the amenities available in Bellevue Square, such as the community center, playground, and local school, which contributed to a self-contained and nurturing environment.
# Community Businesses and Local Economy

> The conversation focuses on local businesses that were central to daily life in Bellevue Square, including Jake's drugstore and Nellie's candy store. The speaker shares memories of working at Nellie's and describes the significance of these establishments, which were run by Black community members. She also mentions informal businesses, such as food vendors, that contributed to the neighborhood's economy and sense of community.
# Family Connections in the Community

> The speaker discusses her family's connections to other local families, specifically the Barlows, who are relatives through her mother's side. She recalls various members of the Barlow family and their relationships with her siblings, noting generational differences and the interconnectedness of families within the Bellevue Square community.
# School Experiences and Friendships

> The speaker reflects on her love of reading and time spent at the local library, as well as her positive experiences at Arsenal School. She fondly remembers her teachers, especially African American educators like Miss Mildred Smith and Miss West, who encouraged academic excellence. The speaker describes the close friendships formed in childhood, the supportive environment, and the sense of safety and unity among families in Bellevue Square.
# Other Community Leaders and Church Life

> The speaker recalls other influential community members, such as Mr. Smith from Parks and Recreation, who ensured children had access to programs and equipment. She describes the family's religious life, attending Saint Michael's Roman Catholic Church and participating in catechism. The speaker emphasizes her mother's role in maintaining religious traditions and the importance of church in their upbringing, reinforcing the values of unity and resilience.
# Transition to Northwest Catholic and Navigating Difference

> The speaker describes her transition from Bellevue Square to attending Northwest Catholic, a predominantly white and affluent school. She discusses the challenges of feeling different due to race and socioeconomic status, the initial difficulties in adjusting, and the support she received from teachers. The experience broadened her perspective, strengthened her resolve to become a teacher, and highlighted the importance of seizing educational opportunities.
# Making Friends and Navigating Race at Northwest Catholic

> The speaker shares her experiences making friends at Northwest Catholic, including both white and Black students. She describes how some white classmates became genuine friends, visiting each other's homes despite societal norms. The speaker also discusses the racial dynamics of the time, the support from a Black Spanish teacher who encouraged her, and the impact of these relationships on her confidence and academic journey.
# Life Beyond Bellevue Square: Shopping, Transportation, and Family Outings

> The conversation shifts to life outside Bellevue Square, with the speaker describing limited excursions beyond the neighborhood due to the self-sufficiency of the community. She recalls family shopping trips in their station wagon, feelings of embarrassment as a child, and family outings to parks during hot summers. These experiences highlight the family's resourcefulness and the importance of togetherness in their daily lives.
# College at Eastern and Personal Growth

> The speaker discusses her transition to college at Eastern, where she participated in a summer program for students with potential. She describes the personal growth she experienced, the challenges of being away from her close-knit family, and early encounters with racism, such as a white roommate refusing to share a room. These experiences helped her develop resilience, a sense of purpose, and a commitment to education and community service.
# Giving Back and Reflections on Lessons from Bellevue Square

> The speaker reflects on her ongoing commitment to giving back to the community, inspired by her upbringing in Bellevue Square. She emphasizes the valuable life lessons learned from growing up in a large family with limited resources, such as cooperation, adaptability, and hope. The speaker expresses pride in her background and the positive impact it has had on her life and career as an educator and community volunteer.
# Wrapping Up and Planning for Future Interviews

> The interview concludes with Dr. Vernal assigning homework and planning for a follow-up session. The speaker is encouraged to gather more family information and involve siblings in future interviews. Both express enthusiasm for documenting and preserving the history of Bellevue Square and the speaker's family, recognizing the importance of oral history in capturing community experiences.

story.title: Reesa Cannada and Dr Fiona Vernal
story.description: Dr. Fiona Vernal interviews Reesa Cannada about her experiences growing up in Bellevue Square, a housing project in Hartford, and her journey to education and teaching. Cannada reflects fondly on her family and memories of childhood, emphasizing the strong sense of community and support despite the financial struggles mentioned in a newspaper article. Her transition to Northwest Catholic High School introduced her to a more diverse environment, contributing to her personal growth. Cannada later attended Eastern where her experiences further shaped her view on education. She continues to work within her community, emphasizing the importance of cultural heritage and family history.
